topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The final notice, stark white against the dingy beige wall of the administrative office, felt like a physical blow. Liam traced the embossed lettering with a trembling finger, the edges of the paper rough beneath his skin. He hadn't even bothered to look at the bulletin board where the campus events were usually plastered, opting instead to stare blankly at the scuffed linoleum floor. Every casual glance from a passing student seemed to land on him, and he felt the familiar tightening in his chest, a dull, heavy ache that made breathing shallow. He just wanted the noise to stop, the expectation to vanish. He slowly folded the notice, the sound of the paper rustling sounding deafening in the sudden silence of his cramped dorm room. He didn't move to throw it away; instead, he tucked it deep into the bottom of his backpack, a small, heavy secret he was now obligated to carry into the empty hallways.

[story 2]

"So, this is it," Sarah mumbled, the words tasting like dust in her mouth. She leaned against the cold brick of the old library, the late afternoon sun doing little to warm the chill that had settled deep in her bones. She watched the students milling about—laughing, gesturing, moving with an easy confidence she couldn't muster. Her own hands felt clumsy, useless, as if they belonged to someone else. She picked at a loose thread on her worn sweater, the repetitive motion a desperate attempt to anchor herself against the swirling emptiness inside. When the official announcement finally registered, it wasn't a shock, but a profound, draining quiet. She simply nodded, a small, almost imperceptible movement, and turned away before anyone could see the shadow that had momentarily crossed her face.

[story 3]

I sat at my desk, the glow of my laptop screen reflecting in my tired eyes. The official email had been sent hours ago, but the reality of the closure felt like a slow-motion disaster unfolding in my periphery. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My shoulders slumped forward, pulling my neck into an uncomfortable curve. I picked up a pen, intending to write a note, but the ink just seemed to smear on the paper. Why bother? What was the point of staying? The silence of the room amplified the frantic rhythm of my own heartbeat, a frantic drum solo against the quiet despair. I let the pen drop onto the desk, the small clatter echoing the complete collapse of whatever structure I had been clinging to.
